content.text: Why Training Measurement Is Not Optional

A training programme that runs without measurement assumes the design was correct the first time. For GenAI adoption in enterprise settings, this assumption is usually wrong. The first cohort reveals gaps in the curriculum -- scenarios that do not reflect the real tool behaviour, module sequences that leave learners without necessary prerequisite knowledge, and assessment methods that test the wrong thing. Without measurement, these gaps persist across every subsequent cohort.

Completion rate is not a training effectiveness metric. It measures whether people showed up. The metric that matters is whether trained employees perform differently -- and better -- in the specific behaviours the training was designed to change.
The Kirkpatrick Framework Adapted for GenAI

attrs.alt: Training Effectiveness Framework -- Kirkpatrick four levels adapted for GenAI

content.text: The Kirkpatrick model defines four levels of training evaluation, from the easiest to collect (learner reaction) to the most meaningful (business results). In GenAI training, the framework maps naturally: Level 1 measures how learners felt about the training; Level 2 measures what they learned; Level 3 measures whether they changed their behaviour in the job; Level 4 measures whether that behaviour change produced the intended business outcome. Most training programmes measure Level 1 and stop there, which tells you whether the training was enjoyable but not whether it was effective.
Defining the Measurement Framework

Each level of the Kirkpatrick model requires a distinct set of metrics, measurement methods, and timing. The metrics must be defined before the training programme begins, not after the first cohort completes, because several of them require baseline data collection that precedes the training itself.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class TrainingMetric:
    metric_name: str
    kirkpatrick_level: int      # 1, 2, 3, or 4
    measurement_method: str
    measurement_timing: str     # when to collect
    amber_threshold: float      # value that triggers review
    red_threshold: float        # value that triggers immediate action
    higher_is_better: bool = True

    def evaluate(self, value: float) -> str:
        if self.higher_is_better:
            if value >= self.amber_threshold:
                return "GREEN"
            elif value >= self.red_threshold:
                return "AMBER -- review curriculum"
            else:
                return "RED -- immediate intervention"
        else:
            if value <= self.amber_threshold:
                return "GREEN"
            elif value <= self.red_threshold:
                return "AMBER -- review curriculum"
            else:
                return "RED -- immediate intervention"


# Define the measurement framework
metrics = [
    # Level 1: Reaction
    TrainingMetric(
        "Learner satisfaction score",
        kirkpatrick_level=1,
        measurement_method="Post-training pulse survey (1-5 scale)",
        measurement_timing="Within 24h of module completion",
        amber_threshold=3.5,
        red_threshold=2.5,
    ),
    TrainingMetric(
        "Perceived job relevance",
        kirkpatrick_level=1,
        measurement_method="Survey Q: 'This training applies directly to how I work' (1-5)",
        measurement_timing="Within 24h of module completion",
        amber_threshold=3.5,
        red_threshold=2.5,
    ),
    # Level 2: Learning
    TrainingMetric(
        "Lab detection score",
        kirkpatrick_level=2,
        measurement_method="Scored simulation: pct of planted errors detected minus false positives",
        measurement_timing="At assessment completion",
        amber_threshold=0.75,
        red_threshold=0.60,
    ),
    TrainingMetric(
        "Pre/post knowledge delta",
        kirkpatrick_level=2,
        measurement_method="Same quiz before and after module; measure score improvement",
        measurement_timing="Before and immediately after foundation module",
        amber_threshold=0.20,   # 20 percentage point improvement
        red_threshold=0.10,
    ),
    # Level 3: Behaviour
    TrainingMetric(
        "Validation checklist compliance",
        kirkpatrick_level=3,
        measurement_method="Audit log: pct of AI-assisted outputs with checklist completion record",
        measurement_timing="2 weeks post go-live, then monthly",
        amber_threshold=0.85,
        red_threshold=0.70,
    ),
    TrainingMetric(
        "AI error report rate",
        kirkpatrick_level=3,
        measurement_method="Incident reports per 1000 AI-assisted outputs",
        measurement_timing="Monthly",
        amber_threshold=2.0,    # too low: under-reporting; check with higher_is_better=False
        red_threshold=0.5,
        higher_is_better=False,  # inverted: 0 reports = no reporting culture, not good quality
    ),
    # Level 4: Results
    TrainingMetric(
        "Production AI error rate",
        kirkpatrick_level=4,
        measurement_method="P1+P2 incidents per 1000 AI-assisted outputs delivered to clients",
        measurement_timing="30d, 90d, 180d post go-live",
        amber_threshold=1.0,
        red_threshold=3.0,
        higher_is_better=False,
    ),
    TrainingMetric(
        "Tool adoption rate",
        kirkpatrick_level=4,
        measurement_method="Pct of certified users who used tool at least weekly in past 30d",
        measurement_timing="Monthly",
        amber_threshold=0.70,
        red_threshold=0.50,
    ),
]

# Simulate a 30-day post go-live evaluation
measurements = {
    "Learner satisfaction score": 4.1,
    "Perceived job relevance": 3.4,
    "Lab detection score": 0.81,
    "Pre/post knowledge delta": 0.32,
    "Validation checklist compliance": 0.88,
    "AI error report rate": 0.3,  # very low -- likely under-reporting
    "Production AI error rate": 0.4,
    "Tool adoption rate": 0.74,
}

print("Training effectiveness evaluation -- 30 days post go-live:")
print()
for level in [1, 2, 3, 4]:
    level_names = {1: "Reaction", 2: "Learning", 3: "Behaviour", 4: "Results"}
    print(f"Level {level}: {level_names[level]}")
    for m in metrics:
        if m.kirkpatrick_level == level and m.metric_name in measurements:
            val = measurements[m.metric_name]
            status = m.evaluate(val)
            print(f"  {m.metric_name}: {val} --> {status}")
    print()
Level 1 Measurement: Learner Reaction

Level 1 data is the easiest to collect and the least predictive of training effectiveness. High satisfaction scores do not predict high detection performance. However, consistently low satisfaction or low perceived relevance scores are leading indicators of future non-engagement and low adoption. The key signals to watch:
Signal
What It Reveals
Action if Red
Satisfaction < 2.5/5
Training experience was poor -- content, delivery, or technical issues
Identify specific modules with low scores; investigate content and delivery issues before next cohort
Perceived relevance < 3.0/5 for a specific role
Scenarios and examples do not resemble that role's actual work
Replace or augment with role-specific examples; consider separate module variants by function
Confidence rating unchanged pre/post
Learners felt no more prepared after training than before
Investigate whether the content matched the actual tool they will use; check for mismatch between training environment and production

Level 1 surveys must be collected immediately after module completion, not at the end of the programme. A learner who found Module 2 confusing may have recovered by Module 4, masking the gap. Granular feedback per module enables targeted fixes.
Level 2 Measurement: Learning

Level 2 measurement answers the question: did the training transfer any knowledge or skill? Two distinct methods are needed for GenAI training: a knowledge quiz (for declarative content like policy and concepts) and a practical assessment (for procedural skills like hallucination detection and validation).
Pre/Post Quiz Delta

Administering the same quiz before and after a foundation module measures the actual knowledge transfer, not just the post-training score. An employee who scores 85% post-training but scored 80% pre-training gained little from the module. An employee who scored 40% pre-training and 85% post-training gained significantly. The delta is the metric; the final score alone is misleading.
Lab Detection Score as the Primary Level 2 Metric

For Practitioner-level training, the lab detection score is the most important Level 2 metric. It directly measures whether the learner can do the thing the training was designed to teach: identify AI errors in realistic outputs. The score should measure both sensitivity (detecting planted errors) and specificity (not flagging correct outputs as errors). A learner who flags everything as suspicious gets a high sensitivity score but a low specificity score -- they would create unworkable amounts of false positives in production.
Level 3 Measurement: Behaviour Transfer

Level 3 is where most training measurement programmes fail. Measuring whether training changed behaviour in the job requires data that is harder to collect than a survey: observation data, audit log data, or structured manager assessment. The most scalable approach for GenAI training is audit log analysis.
Validation Checklist Compliance via Audit Logs

If the AI tool workflow requires learners to complete a validation checklist before marking output as reviewed, the completion rate of that checklist is a direct behavioural measure of whether training transferred. This requires the tool to log checklist completion alongside each output, which must be specified in the tool's technical requirements before go-live.
AI Error Report Submission Rate

This metric is counterintuitive: a very low report rate is a warning sign, not a good sign. An organisation whose employees are using AI tools and submitting zero incident reports is most likely not reporting errors, not producing zero errors. A healthy programme in the first 90 days post go-live should see a rising P3 near-miss report rate as employees build the habit of identifying and reporting anomalies. If the rate is below 0.5 per 1000 AI outputs, the most likely explanation is that the incident reporting process was not trained well enough to be habitual.
Level 4 Measurement: Business Results
Level 4 metrics measure whether the training-enabled behaviour change produced the intended business outcome. For GenAI adoption, the primary Level 4 metrics are:
Production AI error rate
-- P1 and P2 incidents per 1000 AI-assisted outputs delivered. This is the ultimate quality gate. It must be measured at 30, 90, and 180 days post go-live to track trend.
Tool adoption rate

-- the percentage of certified users who used the tool at least once per week in the past 30 days. Low adoption after certification indicates a workflow integration problem or a trust problem -- both of which training can address.
Output quality delta

-- for roles where output quality can be measured (e.g., contract review accuracy, code review defect detection), compare quality before and after AI adoption. This requires a baseline measurement before training begins.
Throughput per person

-- average number of tasks completed per day for AI-assisted roles vs. the pre-adoption baseline. This is a secondary metric; it should not be the primary one, because optimising for throughput at the cost of validation thoroughness is the wrong outcome.
Closing the Loop: Curriculum Iteration

Measurement data is only useful if it drives curriculum change. Each cohort produces a set of Level 1 through 4 signals that should inform the next cohort's training design. The iteration cycle must be systematic: evidence-based gap identification, scoped intervention design, implementation, and validation.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional


class IterationType(Enum):
    CONTENT_CLARIFICATION = "content_clarification"   # wording was unclear
    SCENARIO_REPLACEMENT = "scenario_replacement"     # lab scenario too easy/hard
    MODULE_RESTRUCTURE = "module_restructure"          # learning sequence wrong
    NEW_MODULE = "new_module"                          # gap in coverage
    DELIVERY_CHANGE = "delivery_change"               # sync vs async, timing


@dataclass
class TrainingGap:
    gap_id: str
    description: str
    evidence: str               # metric + value that revealed it
    affected_modules: list[str]
    recommended_iteration: IterationType
    priority: str               # "immediate", "next-cohort", "next-version"
    estimated_effort_hours: float

    def intervention_brief(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"Gap: {self.description}\n"
            f"Evidence: {self.evidence}\n"
            f"Affected modules: {', '.join(self.affected_modules)}\n"
            f"Recommended change: {self.recommended_iteration.value}\n"
            f"Priority: {self.priority} | Effort: {self.estimated_effort_hours}h"
        )


@dataclass
class CurriculumIteration:
    iteration_id: str
    based_on_cohort: str
    gaps_addressed: list[TrainingGap]
    changes_made: list[str]
    iteration_date: date
    a_b_test: bool = False      # did we run an A/B to validate the fix?

    def total_effort(self) -> float:
        return sum(g.estimated_effort_hours for g in self.gaps_addressed)

    def summary(self) -> str:
        immediate = [g for g in self.gaps_addressed if g.priority == "immediate"]
        return (
            f"Iteration {self.iteration_id} ({self.iteration_date})\n"
            f"Based on cohort: {self.based_on_cohort}\n"
            f"Gaps addressed: {len(self.gaps_addressed)} "
            f"({len(immediate)} immediate)\n"
            f"Total effort: {self.total_effort()}h\n"
            f"A/B validation: {'Yes' if self.a_b_test else 'No'}"
        )


# Example: iteration triggered by low Level 2 and Level 3 scores
gaps = [
    TrainingGap(
        gap_id="GAP-001",
        description=(
            "Low AI error report rate (0.3 per 1000) suggests under-reporting, "
            "not good quality. Learners not using the incident channel."
        ),
        evidence="AI error report rate: 0.3 (red threshold: 0.5 -- too low signals under-reporting)",
        affected_modules=["P1", "F2"],
        recommended_iteration=IterationType.CONTENT_CLARIFICATION,
        priority="immediate",
        estimated_effort_hours=2.0,
    ),
    TrainingGap(
        gap_id="GAP-002",
        description=(
            "Perceived job relevance score 3.4 (amber). Learners in the Finance function "
            "report training examples feel like legal scenarios, not their work."
        ),
        evidence="Perceived job relevance: 3.4 (amber: <3.5); Finance cohort comment analysis",
        affected_modules=["P1"],
        recommended_iteration=IterationType.SCENARIO_REPLACEMENT,
        priority="next-cohort",
        estimated_effort_hours=8.0,
    ),
]

iteration = CurriculumIteration(
    iteration_id="ITR-2026-03",
    based_on_cohort="Legal Services + Finance Wave 1 (July 2026)",
    gaps_addressed=gaps,
    changes_made=[
        "P1 module: added explicit step-by-step instructions for submitting incident reports "
        "with a practice exercise (learner submits a mock report in the lab environment)",
        "P1 module: created Finance-specific lab scenario using accounts payable AI outputs "
        "instead of contract extraction",
        "F2 module: updated responsible use policy section to include incident reporting "
        "as a required, non-optional step with named owner",
    ],
    iteration_date=date(2026, 8, 1),
    a_b_test=False,
)

print(iteration.summary())
print()
for gap in iteration.gaps_addressed:
    print(gap.intervention_brief())
    print()
A/B Testing Training Interventions

When a curriculum change is material -- a new scenario, a restructured module, a different assessment method -- the intervention can be validated through A/B testing: running two variants of the module with different cohorts and comparing Level 2 and Level 3 outcomes. This is most practical when cohorts are large enough (typically 30+ learners per variant) to detect meaningful differences and when the next cohort can be split without operational disruption.
The most common interventions worth A/B testing in GenAI training programmes:
Synchronous vs. asynchronous delivery of the same content (does real-time interaction improve Level 2 scores?)
Role-specific vs. generic scenario examples (does matching the scenario to the learner's function improve Level 3 behaviour transfer?)
Assessment at end-of-module vs. assessment 48 hours after module completion (does spaced retrieval improve retention?)
One consolidated lab vs. two shorter labs distributed across the week (does distributed practice improve detection accuracy?)
Common Measurement Failures
Failure
Why It Matters
Fix
Only Level 1 data collected
High satisfaction scores used to declare training successful while error rates remain high in production
Require Level 3 data (audit log compliance) before declaring any cohort go-live ready
Baseline not established before training
Cannot compute pre/post delta or measure outcome change against pre-AI state
Collect baseline metrics (quality, throughput, error rate) in the 4 weeks before training begins
Measurement happens once (at training completion) and stops
Level 3 and 4 outcomes take weeks to appear; one-time measurement misses degradation and improvement
Schedule Level 3 measurement at 2 weeks and 6 weeks post go-live; Level 4 at 30d, 90d, 180d
Low error report rate treated as quality signal
Under-reporting masked; undetected errors accumulate until a P1 incident
Investigate any error report rate below 0.5 per 1000 outputs; treat low rates as a reporting culture problem
Curriculum iterated without A/B validation
Changes assumed to improve outcomes; regression in detection performance goes undetected
For significant changes, run two-cohort A/B; require Level 2 parity or improvement before rolling change to all future cohorts
Summary

Training effectiveness measurement applies the Kirkpatrick four-level framework to GenAI adoption curricula: Level 1 (learner reaction, collected immediately post-module), Level 2 (knowledge and lab detection score, at assessment), Level 3 (validation checklist compliance and error report submission rate, at 2 and 6 weeks post go-live), and Level 4 (production error rate and tool adoption rate, at 30, 90, and 180 days). Completion rate is not an effectiveness metric. The most important Level 2 metric is the lab detection score; the most important Level 3 signal is the AI error report submission rate, where very low values indicate under-reporting, not good quality. Measurement data drives curriculum iteration: each cohort reveals gaps in content, scenario realism, or assessment design that should be addressed before the next cohort begins. Material changes are validated through A/B testing when cohort size permits.
